title: Green oak tool tote
subtitle: A riven oak tool carrier with a through-tenon handle
excerpt: A tool tote in green oak: a rectangular box open at the top, with a central handle rising on a through-tenon post. The joinery is mortise-and-tenon; the tote sides are riven rather than sawn; and the box is assembled green so it dries tight. Carries hand tools, garden trowels, or a set of carving knives.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for mortise-and-tenon joints; tool carrier forms documented in Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) and early-twentieth-century carpentry manuals.

glossaryTerms.definition: Freshly felled or recently riven timber, still high in moisture content. Green oak is worked in this state; the joinery dries and tightens in the assembled tote.
Split along the grain with a froe and maul. Riven oak panels are stronger than sawn panels of the same thickness because the fibres are uncut.
The projecting end of a component shaped to fit into a mortise in another piece.
A two-handled blade pulled toward the body along the workpiece. Used at the shaving horse to true the riven panels to an even thickness.

content.text: The tool tote is green woodwork's answer to a flat-pack box: assembled from
riven
green
oak panels with pegged or wedged joints, it dries into a structure that tightens as the oak seasons. The handle post passes through a mortise in the base panel and is secured by a wedged
tenon
on the underside. This is a structural assembly rather than a display piece; the tote will work in a tool shed or on a kitchen worktop for decades if the oak is well-chosen and the joints are tight.
Grip: both hands on the drawknife handle; foot pressure on the shaving horse pedal. Never make a drawknife stroke toward a body part not protected by the clamp.

Chisel safety: the mallet drives the mortise chisel; the off-hand holds the chisel handle only, clear of the cutting line. Never use a bench chisel as a drift to lever out waste; the bent blade can break.
Froe and beetle: keep the off-hand clear of the blow line. The froe needs beetle force only to start the split; once running, lever rather than beat.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach.
Workspace: stable bench and shaving horse. The riven panels are shaved on the horse; the mortise is chopped on the bench.
The wood

Source a green oak billet 50 cm long and 30 cm in diameter. This yields enough riven panels for a tote 40 cm long, 20 cm wide, and 15 cm deep. Oak splits along the radial face (from pith to bark) most cleanly; the riven panel shows the medullary rays as a silver fleck pattern. If fresh oak is unavailable, ash is a suitable alternative at a lighter weight.
Method

Rive the billet into panels: two long panels for the sides (40 cm by 15 cm by 12 mm), two short panels for the ends (20 cm by 15 cm by 12 mm), one base panel (40 cm by 20 cm by 12 mm), and one handle post (40 cm long, 25 mm square).
True each panel to an even 12 mm thickness at the
shaving horse
with the
drawknife
. The riven face does not need to be perfectly smooth; slight drawknife facets read as hand-craft character.

Chop a 25 mm square mortise through the centre of the base panel with the mortise chisel and mallet: work from both faces, meeting in the middle to avoid break-out. The handle post passes through this mortise.

Shape the handle post to a comfortable oval cross-section for the upper two-thirds (the grip) and leave it square for the lower third (the tenon shoulder). Saw a slot across the tenon end for the wedge; 10 mm deep, perpendicular to the grain.

Assemble the box: peg or nail the side and end panels at the corners (wooden pegs from riven oak are preferable to nails; they will move with the wood as it dries). Drop the handle post through the base mortise from above. Drive a wedge of dry hardwood into the saw slot on the post end beneath the base; the wedge expands the tenon and locks the post.
Leave the assembled tote to dry for 3 to 4 weeks. The green oak panels will shrink and the joints will tighten. Check the wedge tightness after 2 weeks; drive it further if the handle shows movement.
Finishing

Boiled linseed oil on all exterior surfaces; BLO is not food-safe but this is a tool carrier. Apply with a brush, wipe off surplus. Allow 2 to 3 days for BLO to set hard; oil-soaked rags must be spread flat to dry or drowned in water before disposal; they can smoulder as the oil sets.
Variations
Smaller herb-garden tote: 25 cm long, 12 cm wide, for a set of small garden tools or herb scissors.
Divided interior: a riven divider panel running the length of the tote separates the interior into two channels.
Care

Re-oil once a year. If the wedge loosens after a few years, remove the handle post, pare a fresh wedge from a scrap of hardwood, and re-drive. The mortise itself will not have enlarged; the wedge is the wearing component.

items.cause: Wedge not driven far enough; or mortise was cut oversize
items.fix: Drive the wedge further with a mallet tap. If the mortise is oversize, pare a thicker wedge. In extremis, wrap the tenon in a strip of linen soaked in waterproof PVA; the swollen fabric fills the gap permanently.

items.symptom: Panel corner joint opens during drying
items.cause: Green-oak pegs shrinking along with the panels; or pegs not driven tight
items.fix: Re-drive the peg or add a second peg closer to the corner. Use slightly oversized pegs from dry hardwood; dry pegs in wet oak will not shrink with the panel and hold the joint tighter.
